docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
About Go

"Go will be the server language of the future." - Tobias Lütke, Shopify

What is Go?

Go (or Golang) is a modern, open-source programming language created by Google engineers Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson. Launched in 2009 and publicly released in 2012, Go bridges the gap between:

  • High-performance statically typed languages (like C++)
  • Developer-friendly dynamic languages (like Python)

docs.title: Simplicity and Readability
docs.text:
Clean and Simple

  • Only 25 keywords in total
  • Easy to learn and read
  • Built-in gofmt tool for consistent formatting
  • Reduces cognitive load on developers

docs.title: Strong Typing with Inference
docs.text:
Type System

// Explicit typing
var age int = 25

// Type inference
name := "Go"  // Compiler infers string type

Go combines safety with convenience through its smart type system.

docs.title: Excellent Concurrency Support
docs.text:
Built for Modern Computing

GoroutinesChannels

Lightweight threads that make concurrent programming simple: 

go function()  // Runs in background

Type-safe communication between goroutines: 

ch := make(chan string)
ch <- "message"  // Send
msg := <-ch     // Receive

docs.title: Fast Compilation
docs.text:
Speed Features

  • Smart dependency management
  • Optimized build system
  • Quick compile times even for large projects
  • Enhanced developer productivity

docs.title: Garbage Collection
docs.text:
Memory Management

  • Automatic memory management
  • Efficient garbage collector
  • No manual memory allocation/deallocation
  • Optimized for performance

docs.title: Rich Standard Library
docs.text:
Built-in Packages

  • Networking
  • Cryptography
  • HTTP servers
  • JSON/XML processing
  • Testing frameworks
  • And much more!

docs.title: Cross-Platform Compilation
docs.text:
Build Once, Run Anywhere

# Build for Windows from Linux
GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build

# Build for Mac from Linux
GOOS=darwin GOARCH=amd64 go build

docs.title: Perfect Use Cases
docs.text:
Where Go Shines

Cloud NativeNetwork ServicesCLI ToolsMicroservices

  • Powers Docker and Kubernetes
  • Excellent for cloud infrastructure
  • Built for scalability
  • Container-friendly design
  • High-performance servers
  • Real-time applications
  • API services
  • Web servers

// Single binary output
package main
func main() {
    // Your CLI tool
}
- Dependency-free binaries - Cross-platform support - Fast execution - Easy distribution

  • Quick startup time
  • Low memory footprint
  • Built-in HTTP support
  • Easy deployment

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
Go Proverb

"Less is exponentially more" - Rob Pike

Design Principles

  • Simplicity over complexity
  • Clarity over cleverness
  • Practicality over theory
  • Performance without obscurity

Go 1.18+ added generics while maintaining these core values!

docs.title: Community and Ecosystem
docs.text:
Growing Community

BackingMajor Users
  • Supported by Google
  • Open governance model
  • Active community input
  • Regular releases

docs.title: Ready to Start?
docs.text:
Next Steps

  1. Install Go
  2. Try Hello World
  3. Explore the documentation
  4. Join the community

Final Thought

Go represents the future of systems programming - combining performance, simplicity, and developer productivity. Whether you're building microservices, cloud infrastructure, or command-line tools, Go provides the perfect balance of power and ease of use.

docs.title: Go: Hello World!
docs.text:
Overview

Learn how to write and run your first Go program. This guide covers the essential concepts of creating a simple "Hello, World!" application in Go, a modern programming language known for its simplicity and efficiency.

Prerequisites

Before starting, ensure you have:

  • Go installed on your system
  • A text editor or IDE
  • Basic understanding of using the terminal

docs.title: Checking Your Go Installation
docs.text:
First, verify that Go is properly installed on your system by running this command in your terminal:

go version

You should see output similar to this:

Terminal Output
#$ go version 
go1.25.1 X:nodwarf5 linux/amd64

Not Installed?

If you see a "command not found" error, you'll need to download and install Go first.

docs.title: Writing Your First Go Program
docs.text:
Let's create a simple program that prints "Hello, World!" to the console.

Step 1: Create FileStep 2: Add Code

Create a new file named hello.go in your preferred directory.

Copy this code into your hello.go file:

hello.go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}

docs.title: Understanding the Code
docs.text:
Let's break down each component of our program:

Code Structure

Package DeclarationImport StatementMain Function 
package main
  • Every Go program starts with a package declaration
  • main package creates an executable program
  • Other packages create reusable libraries
import "fmt"
  • Imports the formatting package fmt
  • Provides functions for input/output
  • Essential for console interaction
func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}
  • Entry point of the program
  • fmt.Println() prints text and adds a newline
  • Program execution starts here

docs.title: Running Your Program
docs.text:
There are two ways to run your Go program. Let's explore both methods:

Method 1: Direct ExecutionMethod 2: Build & Execute

Use go run to compile and execute in one step: Terminal

go run hello.go

Output
Hello, World!

Quick Development

This method is perfect for development and testing as it's faster for quick iterations.

1️⃣ First, compile the program: Terminal

go build hello.go

2️⃣ Then run the executable: Terminal - Linux/macOS

./hello
Terminal - Windows
hello.exe

Production Use

This method creates an executable file that you can distribute and run without Go installed.

docs.title: Exploring Print Functions
docs.text:
Go provides several ways to output text. Here are some variations of our Hello World program:

Print Variations

Using fmt.PrintfMultiple Print Statements hello.go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Printf("Hello, World!\n")  // Formatted print
}

Tip

Printf allows formatted strings using placeholders like %s, %d, etc.

hello.go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Print("Hello, ")     // Prints without newline
    fmt.Println("World!")    // Prints with newline
}

Tip

Combine Print and Println for flexible output formatting.

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
Congratulations!

You've successfully written and run your first Go program! Here's what you've learned:

  • Setting up Go
  • Creating a Go source file
  • Understanding basic Go syntax
  • Running Go programs
  • Using different print functions

Next Steps

Ready to learn more? Continue exploring:

  • Go data types and variables
  • Control structures (if, for, switch)
  • Functions and packages
  • Error handling
  • Working with modules

docs.title: Understanding the Go Compiler
docs.text:
Overview

Discover how Go transforms your source code into executable programs through its powerful compiler toolchain. This guide extends our Hello World tutorial by diving deep into the compilation process.

Key Points

  • Go is a compiled language
  • Source code → Machine code transformation
  • Multiple ways to build and run programs
  • Optimized for performance

docs.title: Two Ways to Run Go Programs
docs.text:
1. Build Then Run2. Direct Execution 

#$ go build hello.go
./hello
Perfect for production deployments

#$ go run hello.go
Ideal for development

docs.title: Understanding the Compiler
docs.text:
What is the Go Compiler?

The Go compiler transforms your source code into machine-executable programs through a sophisticated pipeline:

graph LR
    A[Source Code] --> B[Parsing]
    B --> C[Type Checking]
    C --> D[Optimization]
    D --> E[Code Generation]
    E --> F[Binary Executable]
    style A fill:#999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
  1. Parsing
    • Reads source code
    • Creates Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
  2. Type Checking
    • Verifies type correctness
    • Ensures type safety
  3. Optimization
    • Improves performance
    • Reduces code size
  4. Code Generation
    • Produces machine code
    • Target platform specific

docs.title: Building with go build
docs.text:
Basic Usage

Command Syntax
go build [options] [packages]
Simple BuildOutput Location 
go build hello.go
  • 🐧 Linux/macOS: hello
  • 🪟 Windows: hello.exe

docs.title: Running Your Program
docs.text:
Executing the Binary

Linux/macOSWindows 
./hello

hello.exe

docs.title: Why Use go build?
docs.text:
Key Benefits

  1. Performance

    • Pre-compiled for speed
    • No runtime compilation

  2. Distribution

    • Share binaries, not source
    • Professional software delivery

  3. Zero Delay

    • No compilation at runtime
    • Instant startup

  4. Self-Contained

    • Statically linked by default
    • No external dependencies

docs.title: Cross-Platform Magic
docs.text:
Build for Any Platform

Cross-Compilation Commands
# 🪟 Build for Windows
GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build hello.go

# 🍎 Build for macOS
GOOS=darwin GOARCH=amd64 go build hello.go

# 🐧 Build for Linux
GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build hello.go

Build once, run anywhere!

docs.title: Quick Development with go run
docs.text:
What is go run?

A powerful command that combines compilation and execution in one step - perfect for development!

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
Behind the Scenes

sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Developer
    participant G as go run
    participant T as Temp Dir
    participant E as Executable
    D->>G: go run hello.go
    G->>T: 1. Create temp dir
    G->>T: 2. Compile program
    T->>E: 3. Create executable
    E->>D: 4. Run & show output
    G->>T: 5. Clean up

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Basic Usage

Command Syntax
go run [options] [go files] [arguments]
Run Single FileRun Multiple Files 

go run hello.go
Output
Hello, World! 


go run main.go utils.go

docs.title: Advantages & Limitations
docs.text:
Advantages

  • Quick & Easy

    • One command does it all
    • Perfect for rapid testing

  • Clean Workspace

    • No leftover binaries
    • Automatic cleanup

  • Fast Development

    • Instant feedback
    • Great for iterations

Limitations

  • Slower Start

    • Compiles each time
    • Not for production

  • No Artifacts

    • No reusable binaries
    • Requires Go installation

docs.title: Build vs Run: Choose Wisely
docs.text:
Feature Comparison

Feature go build go run Output Executable Temporary Speed Faster Slower Usage Production Development Steps Multiple Single Portable Yes Needs Go

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
When to Use What

go buildgo run

  1. Production Deployment

    • Ready for release
    • Final binaries

  2. CLI Tools

    • Distributable utilities
    • Standalone apps

  3. Performance Critical

    • Fast startup needed
    • Resource intensive

  4. Cross-Platform

    • Multiple OS targets
    • Wide distribution

  1. Development

    • Active coding
    • Quick testing

  2. Experiments

    • Code snippets
    • Quick prototypes

  3. Learning

    • Tutorials
    • Examples

  4. Scripts

    • One-time tasks
    • Quick automations

docs.title: Advanced Techniques
docs.text:
Power User Features

Custom Output NameVersion InformationMultiple Files 

go build -o myapp hello.go
Choose your own executable name!

go build -ldflags="-X main.Version=1.0.0" hello.go
Embed version info at compile time!

# Run specific files
go run file1.go file2.go file3.go

# Run entire package
go run .
Handle complex projects!

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Key Takeaways

  1. go build

    • Production ready
    • Distributable binaries
    • Maximum performance

  2. go run

    • Rapid development
    • Quick testing
    • Learning and exploration

  3. Choose Wisely

    • Consider your use case
    • Think about your audience
    • Plan for distribution

Remember

"The right tool for the right job makes all the difference."
